content.text:
Big Data Analytics
Semester:
2  |
Type:
UEF21  |
VHS:
42h  |
Weekly hours:
3h  |
Lecture:
1h30  |
Lab:
1h30
Personal work VHS:
21h  |
Coefficient:
3  |
Credits:
5
Teaching Objectives
This course aims to introduce students to the different types of big data analytics that organisations can use: how big data analysis can help make predictions, understand practices, and operate large-scale analyses.
Prerequisites:
Linear algebra
Course Content (21h)
Chapter 1 – Big Data Fundamentals
Definitions and the "5Vs" (Volume, Variety, Velocity, Veracity, Value)
Business challenges and use cases
Data lifecycle (collection → storage → analysis → visualisation)
Chapter 2 – Architectures and Ecosystems
Distributed storage (HDFS, S3, Data Lake, Lakehouse)
NoSQL databases (key-value, column, document, graph)
Introduction to Hadoop vs Spark vs Flink
Chapter 3 – Data Ingestion and Pipelines
ETL / ELT (Sqoop, NiFi)
Streaming (Kafka, Flume, Kinesis)
Lambda vs Kappa architectures
Chapter 4 – Massive Data Processing
MapReduce paradigm
Spark Core (RDD, DataFrame, Dataset) and Spark Streaming
Optimisations (partitioning, persistence, broadcast)
Chapter 5 – Analytics and Machine Learning at Scale
MLlib / Spark ML
Distributed ML pipelines
Practical cases: clustering (K-means), regression, recommendation
Chapter 6 – Visualisation and Dashboards
Open-source tools (Kibana, Grafana)
Commercial solutions (Tableau, Power BI)
Data storytelling and real-time dashboards
Assessment
Final examination (in person): 60%
Continuous assessment (CC): 40% — tests, personal work
Bibliography
Kleppmann, M. (2017).
Designing data-intensive applications
. O'Reilly Media.
Davenport, T. H., Barth, P., and Bean, R. (2012). How 'big data' is different.
MIT Sloan Management Review
.
Marz, N., and Warren, J. (2015).
Big Data: Principles and best practices of scalable realtime data systems
. Manning Publications.
Reynolds, V. (2016).
Big Data for Beginners
. CreateSpace Independent Publishing Platform.
